skill.name: network-and-relationship-manager
skill.description: Use Rarefriend MCP to search contacts, manage notes and tags, schedule meetings, and connect supported contact sources.
skill.summary: Manage professional relationships through Rarefriend contact, note, tag, calendar, and integration tools.
skill.author: Whitefield-Labs

security_audit.summary: AI review dismissed most static command and weak crypto detections as Markdown formatting and tool names, not executable Ruby or crypto code. Confirmed risks are medium because setup asks users to run an external npm MCP package and grant Rarefriend access to contacts, calendars, and email. No prompt injection or malicious exfiltration evidence found in SKILL.md, references/SETUP.md, or references/TOOLS.md.

medium_findings.title: External MCP Package Execution and Credential Setup
medium_findings.description: The setup instructions tell users to run @rarefriend-ai/mcp through npx and pass Rarefriend client credentials as environment variables. This is a legitimate MCP setup pattern, but it creates supply-chain and credential handling risk if the package or configuration is compromised.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct setup commands and credential-bearing environment variables are present. The context is normal MCP installation, so the risk is operational rather than confirmed malicious.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Relationship, Calendar, and Email Data Access
medium_findings.description: The skill can search and modify contacts, notes, tags, calendar events, and Microsoft email data through Rarefriend integrations. The access appears user-authorized, but the data classes are sensitive and require clear user consent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented tools explicitly cover contacts, notes, calendars, integrations, and Microsoft email search. No evidence shows unauthorized access, but the privacy impact is clear.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Detections Dismissed
low_findings.description: Static detections for weak cryptography occur in Markdown prose and tool reference text. No cryptographic code, hash implementation, cipher selection, or password handling routine is present in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations contain descriptions, setup text, or parameter names rather than crypto operations. This strongly supports a false positive verdict.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Product and Repository Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to rarefriend.com and the Rarefriend GitHub repository. These links are expected for product setup and documentation, with no evidence of covert exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible setup links to the named service and repository. I found no hidden or unrelated network destination in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Ruby Backtick Command Detections Are Mostly Markdown Formatting
low_findings.description: Most external command detections are backticked tool names, parameters, or fenced examples in Markdown. They are not Ruby backtick execution because the skill contains documentation files, not Ruby source code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static pattern is triggered by Markdown code formatting. Some real setup commands exist, but the broad Ruby execution classification is incorrect.

dangerous_patterns.title: npx Execution of Third-Party MCP Server
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation uses npx to run the Rarefriend MCP server package. Users should trust the package source before installing or running it.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The npx command is directly documented in setup instructions. The pattern is common but still executes external package code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local MCP Config Files Store Service Credentials
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup examples place Rarefriend client ID and client secret values in local MCP configuration. File permissions and secret rotation should be considered.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The configuration examples clearly include secret fields. The placeholders are safe, but real deployments would store sensitive values locally.

content.value_statement: Relationship context is hard to track across contacts, notes, calendars, and email. This skill helps assistants use Rarefriend MCP to search, update, and organize professional network data.
actual_capabilities: Search contacts by name, company, role, email, source, or tag.
Create, update, and retrieve Rarefriend contact records.
Create, search, pin, update, and delete relationship notes.
Add, remove, list, and rename tags across contacts.
Schedule, reschedule, cancel, and search Google or Microsoft calendar events.
Start supported Google, Microsoft, Gmail, and LinkedIn-related integration workflows.
limitations: Requires the Rarefriend MCP server and valid Rarefriend client credentials.
LinkedIn sync requires the separate Rarefriend Chrome extension.
Some integrations require OAuth completion in a browser outside the assistant.
Bulk contact creation is limited to 50 contacts per call.

use_cases.title: Find Warm Introductions
use_cases.description: Search your network for contacts at a company and review source attribution before outreach.

use_cases.title: Capture Meeting Context
use_cases.description: Add follow-up notes, pin key details, and search prior discussions before the next meeting.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Relationship Workflows
use_cases.description: Resolve attendee emails, find available calendar time, and create meetings from contact data.

prompt_templates.title: Search My Network
prompt_templates.prompt: Find people I know at Acme and show their role, company, and source.

prompt_templates.title: Add Meeting Notes
prompt_templates.prompt: Add a note for Priya Shah: discussed Q3 partnership goals and should follow up next week.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare for Outreach
prompt_templates.prompt: Search notes and contacts for everyone tagged investor, then summarize who I should contact about our seed round.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Multi-Step Follow-Up
prompt_templates.prompt: Find my contacts at Contoso, identify the best warm introduction, check calendar availability, and draft a follow-up plan.

output_examples.input: Who do I know at Acme?
output_examples.output: Found three Acme contacts. The assistant lists each person with role, source, and the most relevant note before suggesting next steps.

output_examples.input: Log notes from my call with Jordan.
output_examples.output: The assistant finds the matching contact, confirms the current profile, and records the meeting note with important follow-up details.

output_examples.input: Schedule time with Maya next week.
output_examples.output: The assistant resolves Maya’s email from contacts, checks available time, and creates a calendar event after user confirmation.

best_practices: Run setup detection before using CRM tools so missing integrations are clear.
Search contacts before fetching full profiles to avoid unnecessary data exposure.
Use source attribution when presenting contacts from synced systems.
anti_patterns: Do not ask users to paste OAuth credentials into chat.
Do not fetch every full contact profile when a search result list is enough.
Do not bulk import more than 50 contacts in one call.

faq.question: What service does this skill use?
faq.answer: It uses Rarefriend MCP to manage contacts, notes, tags, calendars, and supported integrations.

faq.question: Does it require setup?
faq.answer: Yes. Users need Rarefriend client credentials and the @rarefriend-ai/mcp server configured in their assistant client.

faq.question: Can it connect Google or Microsoft accounts?
faq.answer: Yes. It can start supported integration flows and present an OAuth connection link.

faq.question: Can it manage LinkedIn contacts?
faq.answer: It can search LinkedIn-synced contacts after the Rarefriend Chrome extension has synced them.

faq.question: Can it schedule meetings?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports Google Calendar and Microsoft calendar scheduling when those integrations are connected.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is publishable with a warning because it runs an external MCP package and handles sensitive relationship data.
